1:
"Having therefore these promises, dearly beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God." The witnesses note the call to cleanse from all defilement and perfect holiness. On "having therefore these promises," the grammatical voices note the ground. Barnes and Henry note Paul gathers up the promises just given (6:16-18 — God dwelling among them, being their Father) as the motive: "having these promises," the believer is moved to holiness.

On "cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit," the witnesses note the scope. Barnes and Calvin note the call to "cleanse ourselves" from "all filthiness (defilement, pollution) of the flesh and spirit" — both outward, bodily sins and inward, spiritual ones; the whole person to be purified.

On "perfecting holiness in the fear of God," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the goal: "perfecting (carrying on to completion) holiness" — pressing toward full sanctification — and "in the fear of God," from reverent awe of him. The verse displays the call to cleanse from all defilement and perfect holiness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul gathers up the promises (6:16-18) as the motive — "having these promises," the believer is moved to holiness (Barnes, Henry); the call to "cleanse ourselves" from "all filthiness of the flesh and spirit," both outward and inward sins, the whole person purified (Barnes, Calvin); "perfecting holiness" toward full sanctification, "in the fear of God," from reverent awe (Calvin, Wesley). The verse displays the call to holiness. The verse marks the ground: these promises. The verse displays the cleansing: flesh and spirit. The verse displays the goal: perfecting holiness. The verse displays the motive: the fear of God. The call displays cleansing the whole person toward full holiness. The verse displays the promises: the motive. The verse displays the defilement: cleansed. The verse displays the holiness: perfected. The call to cleanse from all defilement and perfect holiness displays the believer's response to God's promises: moved by them, we are to purify ourselves from every pollution of body and spirit and press on toward complete holiness, in reverent fear of God. The verse displays the pursuit of holiness, opening Paul's appeal for their affection (vv.2-4).
2:
"Receive us; we have wronged no man, we have corrupted no man, we have defrauded no man." The brief verse notes Paul's appeal to be received, with his clear conscience. The grammatical voices note the plea. Barnes and Chrysostom note Paul pleads "receive us" — make room for us in your hearts, give us your full affection and confidence; an appeal for reconciliation and openness.

On "we have wronged no man, corrupted no man, defrauded no man," the witnesses note the vindication. Barnes and Calvin note Paul defends his integrity against the slanders of the false teachers: he has "wronged" (injured) no one, "corrupted" (led astray, in doctrine or morals) no one, "defrauded" (cheated of money) no one — a threefold disavowal.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: Paul's clear conscience is the ground of his appeal; having done them no harm, he may justly ask to be received with affection. The verse displays Paul's appeal to be received, with his clear conscience.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul pleads "receive us" — make room for us in your hearts, an appeal for reconciliation (Barnes, Chrysostom); defending his integrity against slander — he has wronged, corrupted, and defrauded no one, a threefold disavowal (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes his clear conscience is the ground of his appeal. The verse displays the appeal to be received. The verse marks the plea: receive us. The verse displays the vindication: wronged no man. The verse displays the threefold: corrupted, defrauded none. The verse displays the conscience: clear. The appeal displays Paul's integrity grounding his plea for affection. The verse displays the receiving: requested. The verse displays the innocence: of Paul. The verse displays the disavowal: threefold. Paul's appeal to be received, with his clear conscience, displays the ground of his plea for the Corinthians' affection: he asks them to make room for him in their hearts, having wronged, corrupted, and defrauded none — his integrity answering the slanders against him. The verse displays the apostle's vindicated conscience, softened by his deep love (vv.3-4). The threefold denial answers, point for point, the insinuations of the rival teachers, who had hinted that the apostle was either an injurious meddler, a corrupter of the simple, or a man who looked to his own gain; and against all three he sets the plain record of a ministry that had sought nothing but the good of those he served.
3:
"I speak not this to condemn you: for I have said before, that ye are in our hearts to die and live with you." The brief verse notes Paul's deep love, not condemnation, toward the Corinthians. The grammatical voices note the disclaimer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul guards his words: "I speak not this to condemn you" — his vindication (v.2) is not meant as a reproach or accusation against them; he does not wish to wound.

On "ye are in our hearts to die and live with you," the witnesses note the depth of love. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's strong affection: the Corinthians are "in our hearts" — so dear that he is ready "to die and live with" them, bound to them in life and death; an expression of the most devoted love.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the tenderness: Paul's whole appeal flows from love, not censure; he would share life and death with them, so deeply are they in his heart. The verse displays Paul's deep love, not condemnation.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul guards his words — "I speak not this to condemn you," his vindication not a reproach against them (Barnes, Robertson); the Corinthians are "in our hearts," so dear he is ready "to die and live with" them, bound in life and death, the most devoted love (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes his appeal flows from love, not censure. The verse displays Paul's love. The verse marks the disclaimer: not to condemn. The verse displays the affection: in our hearts. The verse displays the devotion: to die and live with you. The verse displays the tenderness: love not censure. The love displays Paul bound to the Corinthians in life and death. The verse displays the guard: not condemning. The verse displays the heart: holding them. The verse displays the bond: life and death. Paul's deep love, not condemnation, toward the Corinthians displays the affection behind his appeal: his vindication is no reproach, for they are so dear to him that he would die and live with them — bound to them in the most devoted love. The verse displays the apostle's tender heart, overflowing in joy and comfort (v.4).
4:
"Great is my boldness of speech toward you, great is my glorying of you: I am filled with comfort, I am exceeding joyful in all our tribulation." The brief verse notes Paul's boldness, glorying, comfort, and joy in the Corinthians. The grammatical voices note the overflow. Barnes and Robertson note Paul pours out his feeling: "great is my boldness (freedom, openness) of speech toward you" — he speaks to them frankly — and "great my glorying (boasting) of you" — he boasts of them to others.

On "filled with comfort... exceeding joyful in all our tribulation," the witnesses note the joy amid affliction. Barnes and Calvin note Paul is "filled with comfort" and "exceeding joyful" — overflowing with joy — and this "in all our tribulation"; his joy in the Corinthians' repentance overrides his many sufferings.

The Reformed Calvin notes the source: the good news of the Corinthians' change (brought by Titus, v.6) so filled Paul with joy that it triumphed over all his troubles. The verse displays Paul's boldness, glorying, comfort, and joy in the Corinthians.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul pours out his feeling — "great is my boldness (openness) of speech toward you" and "great my glorying (boasting) of you" to others (Barnes, Robertson); he is "filled with comfort" and "exceeding joyful," and this "in all our tribulation," his joy overriding his sufferings (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the good news of their change so filled him that it triumphed over his troubles. The verse displays Paul's joy. The verse marks the boldness: of speech. The verse displays the glorying: of them. The verse displays the comfort: filled. The verse displays the joy: amid tribulation. The overflow displays Paul's joy in the Corinthians triumphing over affliction. The verse displays the openness: toward them. The verse displays the boasting: of them. The verse displays the joy: exceeding. Paul's boldness, glorying, comfort, and joy in the Corinthians display the overflow of his affection: he speaks to them frankly and boasts of them to others, filled with comfort and exceeding joyful even in all his tribulation — their repentance triumphing over his troubles. The verse displays the apostle's joy, explained by Titus's arrival and report (vv.5-7).
5:
"For, when we were come into Macedonia, our flesh had no rest, but we were troubled on every side; without were fightings, within were fears." The brief verse notes Paul's restlessness and trouble in Macedonia. The grammatical voices note the affliction. Barnes and Robertson note Paul recounts his distress: when he came into Macedonia (seeking Titus and news of Corinth), "our flesh had no rest" — no relief of body or mind — "but troubled on every side."

On "without were fightings, within were fears," the witnesses note the twofold trouble. Barnes and Henry note the comprehensive distress: "without" (from outward foes, conflicts, persecutions) "were fightings," and "within" (in his own heart) "were fears" — anxiety about the Corinthians and the cause; pressed from without and within.

The Reformed Calvin notes the candor: Paul does not hide his weakness and fears, but freely confesses his anxiety — showing that even the apostle was subject to deep distress before God's comfort came. The verse displays Paul's restlessness and trouble in Macedonia.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: when Paul came into Macedonia (seeking Titus and news), "our flesh had no rest," troubled on every side (Barnes, Robertson); the distress twofold — "without were fightings" (outward conflicts, persecutions), "within were fears" (anxiety about the Corinthians), pressed from without and within (Barnes, Henry). Calvin notes Paul freely confesses his fears, even the apostle subject to distress. The verse displays Paul's trouble. The verse marks the restlessness: no rest. The verse displays the affliction: troubled on every side. The verse displays the fightings: without. The verse displays the fears: within. The trouble displays Paul pressed from without and within. The verse displays the unrest: of his flesh. The verse displays the conflicts: outward. The verse displays the fears: inward. Paul's restlessness and trouble in Macedonia display the depth of his distress before comfort came: arriving anxious for news, he found no rest, pressed on every side — outward conflicts and inward fears — the apostle freely confessing his weakness. The verse displays the affliction that made Titus's coming so welcome (vv.6-7).
6:
"Nevertheless God, that comforteth those that are cast down, comforted us by the coming of Titus;" The brief verse notes God comforting the cast down by the coming of Titus. The grammatical voices note the comfort. Barnes and Robertson note the turn ("nevertheless"): "God, that comforteth those that are cast down (the depressed, the humbled)" — a title of God as the consoler of the dejected — "comforted us by the coming of Titus."

On "by the coming of Titus," the witnesses note the means. Barnes and Calvin note that God's comfort came through Titus's arrival — the long-awaited return of his colleague with news; God used the human means to relieve Paul's distress.

The Reformed Calvin notes the lesson: God is the comforter of the downcast, and he ministers his comfort often through the coming of friends and fellow-workers; Paul ascribes the relief to God, though it came by Titus. The verse displays God comforting the cast down by the coming of Titus.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the turn — "God, that comforteth those that are cast down (the depressed)," a title of God as consoler of the dejected, "comforted us by the coming of Titus" (Barnes, Robertson); God's comfort coming through Titus's arrival, the human means relieving Paul's distress (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes God comforts the downcast, often through the coming of friends, Paul ascribing the relief to God. The verse displays God comforting by Titus. The verse marks the comforter: God of the cast down. The verse displays the means: the coming of Titus. The verse displays the relief: of Paul's distress. The verse displays the lesson: comfort through friends. The comfort displays God consoling the downcast through Titus. The verse displays the God: of comfort. The verse displays the coming: of Titus. The verse displays the relief: granted. God comforting the cast down by the coming of Titus displays the gracious relief of Paul's distress: the God who consoles the dejected sent comfort through the long-awaited arrival of Titus — the divine consolation ministered by a faithful friend. The verse displays the source and means of Paul's comfort, deepened by Titus's report (v.7).
7:
"And not by his coming only, but by the consolation wherewith he was comforted in you, when he told us your earnest desire, your mourning, your fervent mind toward me; so that I rejoiced the more." The brief verse notes the comfort of Titus's good report of the Corinthians. The grammatical voices note the deeper cause. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's comfort came "not by his coming only, but by the consolation wherewith he (Titus) was comforted in you" — Titus himself had been refreshed among them, and his report doubled Paul's joy.

On "your earnest desire, your mourning, your fervent mind toward me," the witnesses note the threefold good news. Barnes and Calvin note Titus told of their "earnest desire" (longing to see Paul, or to set things right), their "mourning" (grief over their fault), and their "fervent mind (zeal) toward me" — tokens of their renewed love and repentance.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the joy: this report of the Corinthians' longing, grief, and zeal made Paul "rejoice the more" — his joy overflowing at their changed hearts. The verse displays the comfort of Titus's good report.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's comfort came "not by his coming only, but by the consolation wherewith Titus was comforted in you" — Titus refreshed among them, his report doubling Paul's joy (Barnes, Robertson); Titus telling of their "earnest desire," "mourning" (grief over their fault), and "fervent mind toward me," tokens of renewed love and repentance (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes this made Paul rejoice the more. The verse displays the comfort of the report. The verse marks the source: Titus comforted in them. The verse displays the news: desire, mourning, zeal. The verse displays the tokens: of repentance. The verse displays the joy: increased. The report displays the Corinthians' renewed love comforting Paul. The verse displays the consolation: of Titus. The verse displays the longing: of the Corinthians. The verse displays the joy: the more. The comfort of Titus's good report of the Corinthians displays the deeper cause of Paul's joy: not Titus's arrival alone, but his news of their longing, grief, and zeal toward Paul — tokens of their renewed love and repentance — made the apostle rejoice the more. The verse displays the gladdening report of repentance, which Paul now reflects upon (vv.8-11).
8:
"For though I made you sorry with a letter, I do not repent, though I did repent: for I perceive that the same epistle hath made you sorry, though it were but for a season." The witnesses note Paul's reflection on the sorrow his letter caused. On "though I made you sorry with a letter, I do not repent, though I did repent," the grammatical voices note the change of feeling. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's frank disclosure: his former letter (the severe one) "made them sorry"; he had for a time "repented" (regretted) sending it, fearing it too harsh, but now "does not repent" — seeing its good effect.

On "I perceive the same epistle made you sorry, though but for a season," the witnesses note the temporary grief. Barnes and Chrysostom note that the sorrow the letter caused was "but for a season" (temporary, transient) — not a lasting wound, but a passing grief that led to repentance.

The Reformed Calvin notes the wisdom: Paul's momentary regret shows his tender heart, but the outcome justified the severity; the brief sorrow worked a lasting good. The verse displays Paul's reflection on the sorrow his letter caused.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's frank disclosure — his former (severe) letter made them sorry, and he had for a time regretted sending it, fearing it too harsh, but now does not, seeing its good effect (Barnes, Robertson); the sorrow it caused "but for a season," a passing grief that led to repentance (Barnes, Chrysostom). Calvin notes his momentary regret shows his tender heart, but the outcome justified the severity. The verse displays Paul's reflection. The verse marks the sorrow: caused by the letter. The verse displays the regret: once felt, now gone. The verse displays the grief: temporary. The verse displays the wisdom: severity justified. The reflection displays the brief sorrow working a lasting good. The verse displays the letter: making sorry. The verse displays the regret: passed. The verse displays the grief: for a season. Paul's reflection on the sorrow his letter caused displays his tender yet wise pastoral heart: though he had for a time regretted the severity that grieved them, he no longer does, for the sorrow was brief and bore good fruit. The verse displays the apostle's vindicated severity, explained by the kind of sorrow it produced (vv.9-10).
9:
"Now I rejoice, not that ye were made sorry, but that ye sorrowed to repentance: for ye were made sorry after a godly manner, that ye might receive damage by us in nothing." The brief verse notes Paul's joy not in their sorrow but in their repentance. The grammatical voices note the distinction. Barnes and Robertson note Paul carefully clarifies: "I rejoice, not that ye were made sorry" (he takes no pleasure in their pain) "but that ye sorrowed to repentance" — his joy is in the result, their turning, not the grief itself.

On "made sorry after a godly manner," the witnesses note the kind. Barnes and Calvin note the sorrow was "after a godly manner" (according to God's will) — the right kind of sorrow, the kind that leads to repentance and life, not the worldly kind.

On "that ye might receive damage by us in nothing," the witnesses note the benefit: Paul's letter, far from harming them, did them only good; they suffered no real loss by it. The verse displays Paul's joy not in their sorrow but in their repentance.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul clarifies — "I rejoice, not that ye were made sorry" (no pleasure in their pain) "but that ye sorrowed to repentance," his joy in the result not the grief (Barnes, Robertson); the sorrow "after a godly manner" (according to God's will), the right kind that leads to life (Barnes, Calvin); and they received "damage in nothing," his letter doing only good (Barnes). The verse displays Paul's joy in their repentance. The verse marks the joy: not in sorrow. The verse displays the result: sorrow to repentance. The verse displays the kind: godly. The verse displays the benefit: no damage. The joy displays Paul rejoicing in the godly sorrow that saves. The verse displays the sorrow: to repentance. The verse displays the manner: godly. The verse displays the good: no harm. Paul's joy not in their sorrow but in their repentance displays the rightly-ordered gladness of the pastor: he takes no pleasure in their pain but in its fruit — a godly sorrow that led to repentance and did them no harm but only good. The verse displays the saving sorrow, defined against worldly grief (v.10).
10:
"For godly sorrow worketh repentance to salvation not to be repented of: but the sorrow of the world worketh death." The witnesses note the contrast of godly sorrow and worldly sorrow. On "godly sorrow worketh repentance to salvation," the grammatical voices note the saving grief. Barnes and Robertson note "godly sorrow" (sorrow according to God — that regards sin as committed against God, and leads the soul to him) "worketh (produces) repentance" — a true change of mind and life — "to salvation," and this repentance is "not to be repented of" (gives no cause for regret, is permanent).

On "the sorrow of the world worketh death," the witnesses note the deadly grief. Barnes and Calvin note "the sorrow of the world" — grief arising from worldly causes (loss, shame, fear of consequences), not from sin against God, and not leading to him — "worketh death": it leads to despair, ruin, and (as in Judas) destruction.

The Reformed Calvin notes a careful guard: repentance is the way to salvation, not its ground or cause — God pardons by free mercy, yet never without repentance; the two are inseparable fruits of grace. The verse displays the contrast of godly and worldly sorrow.

The witnesses agree the great contrast: "godly sorrow" (according to God — regarding sin as against God and leading to him) "worketh repentance to salvation," a change of mind and life that gives no cause for regret; while "the sorrow of the world" (grief from loss, shame, or fear, not from sin against God) "worketh death" — despair and ruin, as in Judas (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). Calvin adds the guard that repentance is the way to salvation, not its ground — God pardons by free mercy, yet never without repentance. The verse displays godly vs worldly sorrow. The verse marks the godly: repentance to salvation. The verse displays the worldly: death. The verse displays the difference: toward God or not. The verse displays the guard: the way, not the ground. The contrast displays the two sorrows leading to life or death. The verse displays the godly sorrow: saving. The verse displays the worldly sorrow: deadly. The verse displays the repentance: not regretted. The contrast of godly sorrow and worldly sorrow displays the decisive difference in grief: sorrow according to God, regarding sin as committed against him, works a repentance unto salvation never to be regretted; while the sorrow of the world, terminating in loss and shame without God, works only death. The verse displays the nature of saving repentance, evidenced in the Corinthians' fruits (v.11).
11:
"For behold this selfsame thing, that ye sorrowed after a godly sort, what carefulness it wrought in you, yea, what clearing of yourselves, yea, what indignation, yea, what fear, yea, what vehement desire, yea, what zeal, yea, what revenge! In all things ye have approved yourselves to be clear in this matter." The witnesses note the seven fruits of the Corinthians' godly sorrow. On "what carefulness it wrought," the grammatical voices note the proof. Barnes and Robertson note Paul appeals to their own case as proof that godly sorrow produces real change, enumerating seven fruits: "carefulness" (earnest diligence) to set things right.

On the series, the witnesses note each fruit. Barnes, Clarke, and Calvin note the seven: "clearing of yourselves" (vindication, eagerness to be rid of blame), "indignation" (against the sin), "fear" (of God's displeasure, or of Paul's rod), "vehement desire" (longing, to amend or to see Paul), "zeal" (fervent earnestness), and "revenge" (just punishment of the offender) — all marks of true repentance.

On "approved yourselves to be clear in this matter," the witnesses note the result: by these fruits the Corinthians had cleared themselves of further blame, having done all they could to remove the evil. The verse displays the seven fruits of godly sorrow.

The witnesses agree Paul appeals to the Corinthians' own case as proof that godly sorrow works real change, enumerating seven fruits: "carefulness" (earnest diligence), "clearing of yourselves" (vindication, eagerness to be rid of blame), "indignation" (against the sin), "fear" (of God's displeasure or Paul's rod), "vehement desire" (longing to amend or to see Paul), "zeal" (fervent earnestness), and "revenge" (just punishment of the offender) — all marks of true repentance (Barnes, Clarke, Calvin); by which they "approved themselves clear in this matter," having done all to remove the evil. The verse displays the seven fruits. The verse marks the proof: their own case. The verse displays the fruits: seven marks. The verse displays the result: cleared themselves. The verse displays the repentance: genuine. The fruits display godly sorrow proved by its effects. The verse displays the carefulness: diligence. The verse displays the indignation: against sin. The verse displays the zeal: fervent. The seven fruits of the Corinthians' godly sorrow display the genuineness of their repentance: their carefulness, self-clearing, indignation, fear, longing, zeal, and just punishment of the offender all proved that their sorrow was according to God — and by them they cleared themselves of further blame. The verse displays the evidences of true repentance, showing Paul's true aim in writing (v.12).
12:
"Wherefore, though I wrote unto you, I did it not for his cause that had done the wrong, nor for his cause that suffered wrong, but that our care for you in the sight of God might appear unto you." The witnesses note Paul's true aim in writing — his care for the whole church. On "not for his cause that had done the wrong, nor for his cause that suffered wrong," the grammatical voices note the disclaimer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul states his deeper purpose: he wrote his severe letter not merely for the sake of the offender (the wrongdoer) nor the one wronged (the injured party), but for a larger end.

On "that our care for you in the sight of God might appear," the witnesses note the real aim. Barnes and Henry note Paul's true purpose: that his "care (earnest concern) for you" — for the whole church's good and purity — might be made manifest, and this "in the sight of God," sincerely and before him.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: Paul's discipline aimed not at individuals merely but at the welfare of the entire church; his severity flowed from pastoral love for them all. The verse displays Paul's true aim in writing — his care for the whole church.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul wrote his severe letter not merely for the sake of the offender (the wrongdoer) nor the one wronged, but for a larger end (Barnes, Robertson); his true purpose that his "care for you" — for the whole church's good and purity — might appear, and this "in the sight of God," sincerely before him (Barnes, Henry). Calvin notes his discipline aimed at the welfare of the entire church, flowing from pastoral love. The verse displays Paul's true aim. The verse marks the disclaimer: not for the parties only. The verse displays the aim: care for you. The verse displays the sincerity: in the sight of God. The verse displays the love: for the whole church. The aim displays Paul's care for the whole church's good. The verse displays the writing: with purpose. The verse displays the care: for them all. The verse displays the sincerity: before God. Paul's true aim in writing — his care for the whole church — displays the larger purpose behind his severity: he wrote not merely for the offender or the offended, but that his earnest, God-witnessed concern for the welfare of the whole church might be made plain. The verse displays the pastoral love behind the discipline, crowned by Paul's joy in their response (vv.13-16).
13:
"Therefore we were comforted in your comfort: yea, and exceedingly the more joyed we for the joy of Titus, because his spirit was refreshed by you all." The brief verse notes Paul's added joy at the refreshing of Titus. The grammatical voices note the shared comfort. Barnes and Robertson note Paul "was comforted in your comfort" — the Corinthians' own consolation (their being set right) was his comfort — and he rejoiced "exceedingly the more for the joy of Titus."

On "his spirit was refreshed by you all," the witnesses note Titus's refreshment. Barnes and Chrysostom note that Titus's "spirit was refreshed by you all" — the warm reception and changed conduct of the Corinthians had revived and gladdened Titus; and Paul's joy was multiplied by his colleague's joy.

The Reformed Calvin notes the fellowship: the mutual joy of apostle, colleague, and church shows the bond of love among them; Paul rejoices the more because Titus too was gladdened. The verse displays Paul's added joy at the refreshing of Titus.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "was comforted in your comfort" — the Corinthians' own consolation was his comfort — and rejoiced "exceedingly the more for the joy of Titus" (Barnes, Robertson); Titus's "spirit refreshed by you all," revived by their warm reception and changed conduct, multiplying Paul's joy (Barnes, Chrysostom). Calvin notes the mutual joy shows the bond of love. The verse displays Paul's added joy. The verse marks the comfort: in their comfort. The verse displays the joy: for Titus. The verse displays the refreshing: of Titus's spirit. The verse displays the fellowship: mutual joy. The joy displays Paul gladdened the more by Titus's gladness. The verse displays the comfort: shared. The verse displays the refreshing: of Titus. The verse displays the bond: of love. Paul's added joy at the refreshing of Titus displays the multiplying of his comfort: glad in the Corinthians' own consolation, he rejoiced the more that Titus's spirit had been refreshed among them — the mutual joy of apostle, colleague, and church a token of their bond of love. The verse displays the overflow of shared joy, vindicating Paul's boasting (v.14).
14:
"For if I have boasted any thing to him of you, I am not ashamed; but as we spake all things to you in truth, even so our boasting, which I made before Titus, is found a truth." The brief verse notes that Paul's boasting of the Corinthians proved true. The grammatical voices note the vindication. Barnes and Robertson note Paul had "boasted" to Titus of the Corinthians (before sending him), and now "is not ashamed" — for his boast proved well-founded; their conduct vindicated his confidence.

On "as we spake all things to you in truth, so our boasting... is found a truth," the witnesses note the consistency. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's integrity: just as all he had spoken to the Corinthians was true, so his boasting of them to Titus was likewise found true — his word reliable in both directions.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: Paul's honesty is consistent; he neither flattered the Corinthians nor misrepresented them to Titus, and the event proved his word trustworthy. The verse displays that Paul's boasting of the Corinthians proved true.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul had "boasted" to Titus of the Corinthians and now "is not ashamed," his boast proving well-founded, their conduct vindicating his confidence (Barnes, Robertson); his integrity consistent — as all he spoke to them was true, so his boasting of them to Titus was found true (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes Paul neither flattered nor misrepresented them, the event proving his word trustworthy. The verse displays Paul's boast proved true. The verse marks the boasting: of the Corinthians. The verse displays the vindication: not ashamed. The verse displays the consistency: truth in all. The verse displays the integrity: reliable word. The boasting displays Paul's confidence in the Corinthians vindicated. The verse displays the boast: to Titus. The verse displays the truth: confirmed. The verse displays the integrity: of Paul. That Paul's boasting of the Corinthians proved true displays the vindication of his confidence and the consistency of his word: his boast to Titus was not put to shame, for, as all he spoke to them was true, so his boasting of them was found true by the event. The verse displays the apostle's trustworthy word, deepening Titus's love for them (v.15).
15:
"And his inward affection is more abundant toward you, whilst he remembereth the obedience of you all, how with fear and trembling ye received him." The brief verse notes Titus's deepened affection for the obedient Corinthians. The grammatical voices note the love. Barnes and Robertson note that Titus's "inward affection (tender love, literally his bowels, the seat of affection) is more abundant toward you" — his love for the Corinthians had grown, deepened by his experience among them.

On "the obedience of you all, how with fear and trembling ye received him," the witnesses note the cause. Barnes and Calvin note that Titus remembered "the obedience of you all" — their ready compliance — and how they "received him with fear and trembling" (with deep reverence, anxious care to do right, humble respect for his message); this won his heart.

The Reformed Calvin notes the lesson: the Corinthians' humble, obedient reception of Titus and his message drew out his abundant love; reverent obedience binds hearts together. The verse displays Titus's deepened affection for the obedient Corinthians.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Titus's "inward affection (tender love) is more abundant toward you," his love deepened by his experience among them (Barnes, Robertson); he remembered "the obedience of you all" and how they "received him with fear and trembling" (deep reverence, anxious care to do right), which won his heart (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes their humble obedience drew out his love. The verse displays Titus's deepened affection. The verse marks the love: more abundant. The verse displays the cause: their obedience. The verse displays the reception: fear and trembling. The verse displays the lesson: reverence binds hearts. The affection displays Titus's love drawn out by their obedience. The verse displays the love: deepened. The verse displays the obedience: of all. The verse displays the reverence: of their reception. Titus's deepened affection for the obedient Corinthians displays the fruit of their humble repentance: remembering their ready obedience and the reverent fear with which they received him, Titus's love for them grew abundant — reverent obedience binding hearts in mutual affection. The verse displays the love won by obedience, sealing Paul's confidence (v.16).
16:
"I rejoice therefore that I have confidence in you in all things." The brief verse notes Paul's restored confidence in the Corinthians. The grammatical voices note the conclusion. Barnes and Robertson note Paul closes the section with joy: "I rejoice therefore that I have confidence (full trust, good courage) in you in all things" — his confidence in them, once shaken, is now fully restored.

On "in all things," the witnesses note the completeness. Barnes notes the comprehensiveness: not in some matters only but "in all things" Paul now trusts them — their repentance and obedience have won back his entire confidence.

The grammatical-historical note the transition: this restored confidence becomes the ground on which Paul proceeds, in the next chapters, to urge upon them the collection for the saints. The verse displays Paul's restored confidence in the Corinthians.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul closes with joy — "I rejoice therefore that I have confidence (full trust, good courage) in you in all things," his confidence once shaken now fully restored (Barnes, Robertson); "in all things," not in some matters only, their repentance and obedience winning back his entire trust (Barnes). The grammatical-historical note this becomes the ground for urging the collection in the next chapters. The verse displays Paul's restored confidence. The verse marks the joy: I rejoice. The verse displays the confidence: in all things. The verse displays the restoration: trust regained. The verse displays the transition: to the collection. The confidence displays Paul's full trust in the Corinthians restored. The verse displays the rejoicing: of Paul. The verse displays the trust: complete. The verse displays the ground: for what follows. Paul's restored confidence in the Corinthians displays the happy close of the reconciliation: his trust in them, once shaken, is now fully and joyfully restored in all things — a confidence that becomes the ground for the appeal he is about to make. The verse displays the apostle's regained assurance, opening the way to the grace of giving (ch. 8). The note of confidence on which the chapter closes is not mere relief at a quarrel mended but the deliberate ground on which the apostle now ventures to lay before them the great work of the collection, knowing that a church so thoroughly reconciled will not fail him in a matter that touches the love and unity of the whole body of Christ.
